etymology.description: 看(看) (kàn, "look; see") + 一(一) (yī, "one; once") + 看(看) (kàn, "look; see") = take a look
etymology.interpretation: 看(看) (kàn, "look; see") refers to using one's eyes to observe or inspect. 一(一) (yī, "one; once") serves to indicate a single action or an attempt, similar to saying 'once' or 'a bit' in English. By having 看(看) (kàn, "look; see") both before and after 一(一) (yī, "one"), it emphasizes or softens the action, suggesting a brief or casual glance rather than a thorough examination. It carries the sense of 'taking a moment to see', implying a light or simple action with no deep commitment.

examples.chinese: 你可以过来这儿看一看(你可以過來這兒看一看)。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ kěyǐ guòlái zhè'er kàn yī kàn.
examples.english: You can come over here to take a look.

examples.chinese: 我们去书店看一看(我們去書店看一看)，然后再决定(然後再決定)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen qù shūdiàn kàn yī kàn, ránhòu zài juédìng.
examples.english: Let's go to the bookstore to take a look, then decide.

examples.chinese: 她想去市场看一看(她想去市場看一看)。
examples.pinyin: Tā xiǎng qù shìchǎng kàn yī kàn.
examples.english: She wants to go to the market to take a look.
